hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Lead with the highest-confusion cohort: users with partial installs, who reported not knowing whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait—support specifically requested a recovery message that starts with symptoms and then offers one safe next action.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly says partial installs were the biggest source of confusion, users were unsure whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait for a patched CLI, and support wanted a recovery message that starts with symptoms and gives one safe next action.

claimJudgments.claimText: Do not claim universal resolution yet: engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux, while Windows steps are still being validated and include an unresolved credential-cache issue; the word “resolved” was flagged as too broad unless the environment caveat stays highly visible.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly supports all elements: macOS/Linux verified, Windows still validating, unresolved Windows credential-cache issue, and “resolved” flagged as too broad without a visible environment caveat.

claimJudgments.claimText: Structure the briefing into three explicit blocks—who is affected, what action to take, and which environments are pending validation—and keep the Windows caveat in the first paragraph to reduce the risk customers forward the update as proof of universal recovery.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 says customer success wanted the message to separate affected users, recommended action, and pending validation, with the Windows caveat in the first paragraph because customers might forward the update as proof of universal recovery.

claimJudgments.claimText: Recommend the patched CLI recovery command as the safe action because it checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state; note it is available in a release candidate, while public docs/screenshots still reflect the old installer flow and engineering requested docs updates before broad promotion.
missingEvidence: S1 or S6 is needed to support the framing that the command is the single “safe” action; S4 supports what the command does but does not use that safety framing.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 supports the patched CLI recovery command, stale-credential checks, partial-install cleanup, release-candidate availability, outdated screenshots/docs, and engineering’s request for docs updates before broad promotion. It does not by itself establish the “safe action” framing.

claimJudgments.claimText: Mirror support’s OS segmentation (verified macOS, verified Linux, pending Windows) and ensure communications do not point users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted; docs updates (including Windows screenshots) are part of the checklist and Windows screenshots are still marked pending.
claimJudgments.explanation: S6 supports the OS segmentation into verified macOS, verified Linux, and pending Windows. S5 supports avoiding the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted and says the docs checklist includes screenshots, with Windows screenshots pending.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well aligned with the packet: segment by OS and partial-install symptoms, give one safe recovery action, avoid blanket resolved language until Windows is verified, keep the Windows caveat prominent, and avoid routing users to the old installer flow as the release candidate/docs transition proceeds. The release-candidate/docs caveat is mostly handled by the briefing and open questions.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in a partially supported or unsupported claim judgment.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S6 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
